showFullCode.title: Show Complete File
showFullCode.description: Show complete file for code, with bootstrap and error handling included.

bitly_url.description: The unique bit.ly URL for this GIF

create_datetime.description: The date this GIF was added to the GIPHY database.

embded_url.description: A URL used for embedding this GIF

featured_tags.description: An array of featured tags for this GIF (Note: Not available when using the Public Beta Key)

import_datetime.description: The creation or upload date from this GIF's source.

rating.description: The MPAA-style rating for this content. Examples include Y, G, PG, PG-13 and R

slug.description: The unique slug used in this GIF's URL

source.description: The page on which this GIF was found

source_post_url.description: The URL of the webpage on which this GIF was found.

source_tld.description: The top level domain of the source URL.

tags.description: An array of tags for this GIF (Note: Not available when using the Public Beta Key)

trending_datetime.description: The date on which this gif was marked trending, if applicable.

update_datetime.description: The date on which this GIF was last updated.

url.description: The unique URL for this GIF

username.description: The username this GIF is attached to, if applicable

frames.description: The number of frames in this GIF.

height.description: The height of this GIF in pixels.

mp4.description: The URL for this GIF in .MP4 format.

mp4_size.description: The size in bytes of the .MP4 file corresponding to this GIF.

size.description: The size of this GIF in bytes.

url.description: The publicly-accessible direct URL for this GIF.

webp.description: The URL for this GIF in .webp format.

webp_size.description: The size in bytes of the .webp file corresponding to this GIF.

width.description: The width of this GIF in pixels.

Meta.title: Meta
Meta.description: The Meta Object contains basic information regarding the request, whether it was successful, and the response given by the API.  Check `responses` to see a description of types of response codes the API might give you under different cirumstances.

response_id.description: A unique ID paired with this response from the API.

Pagination.title: Pagination
Pagination.description: The Pagination Object contains information relating to the number of total results available as well as the number of results fetched and their relative positions.

User.title: User
User.description: The User Object contains information about the user associated with a GIF and URLs to assets such as that user's avatar image, profile, and more.

avatar_url.description: The URL for this user's avatar image.

banner_url.description: The URL for the banner image that appears atop this user's profile page.

display_name.description: The display name associated with this user (contains formatting the base username might not).

profile_url.description: The URL for this user's profile.

twitter.description: The Twitter username associated with this user, if applicable.

username.description: The username associated with this user.

Images.title: Images
Images.description: An object containing data for various available formats and sizes of this GIF.

Type.title: Type
Type.description: Type of the gif. By default, this is almost always gif

Nodes.Text: Giphy API
Official Giphy Documentation: [https://developers.giphy.com/docs/](https://developers.giphy.com/docs/)
The generated code depends on a few Ruby gems. The references to these gems are added in the gemspec file. The easiest way to resolve the dependencies, build the gem and install it is through Rake:

1. Install Rake if not already installed: `gem install rake`
  2. Install Bundler if not already installed: `gem install bundler`
  3. From terminal/cmd navigate to the root directory of the SDK.
  4. Invoke: `rake install`
Alternatively, you can build and install the gem manually:

1. From terminal/cmd navigate to the root directory of the SDK.
  2. Run the build command: `gem build giphy_api.gemspec`
  3. Run the install command: `gem install giphy_api-1.0.0.gem`

The following section explains how to use the giphy_api ruby gem in a new Rails project using RubyMine™. The basic workflow presented here is also applicable if you prefer using a different editor or IDE.
Close any existing projects in RubyMine™ by selecting `File -> Close Project`. Next, click on `Create New Project` to create a new project from scratch.

Nodes.AlternateText: Create a new project in RubyMine - Step 1

Nodes.Text: Next, provide `TestApp` as the project name, choose `Rails Application` as the project type, and click `OK`.

Nodes.AlternateText: Create a new Rails Application in RubyMine - Step 2

Nodes.Text: In the next dialog make sure that the correct Ruby SDK is being used (>= 2.6 and <= 3.2) and click `OK`.

Nodes.AlternateText: Create a new Rails Application in RubyMine - Step 3

Nodes.Text: In order to use the `giphy_api` gem in the new project we must add a gem reference. Locate the `Gemfile` in the Project Explorer window under the `TestApp` project node. The file contains references to all gems being used in the project. Here, add the reference to the library gem by adding the following line: `gem 'giphy_api', '1.0.0'`

Nodes.AlternateText: Add new reference to the Gemfile

Nodes.Text: Once the `TestApp` project is created, a folder named `controllers` will be visible in the *Project Explorer* under the following path: `TestApp > app > controllers`. Right click on this folder and select `New -> Run Rails Generator...`.

Nodes.AlternateText: Run Rails Generator on Controllers Folder

Nodes.Text: Selecting the said option will popup a small window where the generator names are displayed. Here, select the `controller` template.
Next, a popup window will ask you for a Controller name and included Actions. For controller name provide `Hello` and include an action named `Index` and click `OK`.
A new controller class named `HelloController` will be created in a file named `hello_controller.rb` containing a method named `Index`.

1. Add the `require 'giphy_api'` statement to require the gem in the controller file.
  2. Add the `include GiphyApi` statement to include the sdk module in the controller file.
  3. In the `Index` method, add code for initialization and a sample for its usage.
The following parameters are configurable for the API Client:

Data: The Faraday connection object passed by the SDK user for making requests
The Faraday adapter object passed by the SDK user for performing http requests

The value to use for connection timeout.
**Default: 60**

The number of times to retry an endpoint call if it fails.
**Default: 0**

The amount to multiply each successive retry's interval amount by in order to provide backoff.
**Default: 2**

A list of HTTP methods to retry.
**Default: %i[get put get put]**
The Http CallBack allows defining callables for pre and post API calls.
Optional proxy configuration to route HTTP requests through a proxy server.
The credential object for Custom Query Parameter

Nodes.Text: The API client can be initialized as follows:
Code-Based Client Initialization

require 'giphy_api'
include GiphyApi

client = Client.new(
  custom_query_authentication_credentials: CustomQueryAuthenticationCredentials.new(
    api_key: 'api_key'
  )
)
Environment-Based Client Initialization

require 'giphy_api'
include GiphyApi

# Create client from environment
client = Client.from_env
See the [```Environment-Based Client Initialization```]($h/__additional_documentation/Configuration/Environment-Based%20Client%20Initialization) section for details.

This API uses the following authentication schemes.
* [`api_key (Custom Query Parameter)`]($h/__auth_CustomQueryAuthentication)
Documentation for accessing and setting credentials for api_key.
**Note:** Auth credentials can be set using named parameter for any of the above credentials (e.g. `api_key`) in the client initialization.
You must provide credentials in the client as shown in the following code snippet.

require 'giphy_api'
include GiphyApi

client = Client.new(
  custom_query_authentication_credentials: CustomQueryAuthenticationCredentials.new(
    api_key: 'api_key'
  )
)
To run the tests, navigate to the root directory of the SDK in your terminal and execute the following command:
rake
Giphy API Client
The gateway for the SDK. This class acts as a factory for the Controllers and also holds the configuration of the SDK.
Controllers

* [Gifs]($e/gifs)
* [Stickers]($e/stickers)
An instance of the `GifsController` class can be accessed from the API Client.
gifs_controller = client.gifs

Nodes.Title: Get Gifs by Id
Nodes.Name: get_gifs_by_id
Nodes.Description: A multiget version of the get GIF by ID endpoint.

Parameters.Name: ids
Parameters.Description: Filters results by specified GIF IDs, separated by commas.

Errors.Name: APIException

Nodes.Text: Represents the proxy server configurations for API calls.
Properties

Data: The port to connect to the proxy server.

CodeExamples.Title: Usage Example
CodeExamples.Text:
require 'giphy_api'
include GiphyApi


client = GiphyApi::Client.new(
  proxy_settings: ProxySettings.new(
    address: "http://localhost",
    port: 8888,
    username: 'user',
    password: 'pass'
  )
)

Nodes.Text: The SDK client can also be initialized directly from environment variables using the `from_env` class method. This allows the SDK to automatically read configuration values from the runtime environment or a `.env` file.

require 'giphy_api'
include GiphyApi

# Create client from environment
client = Client.from_env

You can also load values from a `.env` file before creating the client.
To do this, install and use the dotenv
 gem:
gem install dotenv

Now require 'dotenv/load' automatically loads all variables from a `.env` file into `ENV`,
so the `from_env` method can access them.

require 'dotenv/load'
require 'giphy_api'
include GiphyApi

# Create client from environment
client = Client.from_env
The same method can accept keyword arguments to override any values read from the environment.

# To override or fill in values that are not set in the environment, pass them
# as keyword arguments when calling `from_env`
client = Client.from_env(environment: 'production', timeout: 30)
Values provided through arguments take precedence over those defined in environment variables.
Example .env File

ENVIRONMENT='production'

API_KEY='apiKey'

TIMEOUT=60
MAX_RETRIES=3
BACKOFF_FACTOR=2
RETRY_INTERVAL=1
RETRY_STATUSES=408,413
RETRY_METHODS=GET,PUT,DELETE
PROXY_ADDRESS='http://localhost:3000'
PROXY_PORT=8080
PROXY_USERNAME='username'
PROXY_PASSWORD='password'
Note
- If an environment variable is not defined, the default SDK configuration value will be used.
